attributes.stem: A 24-year-old medical student presents to the General Practice clinic for a routine consultation. Three days ago, her grandmother, with whom she was very close, died from metastatic breast cancer. When asked how she is coping, she responds: "I've been reviewing the literature on BRCA mutations and their role in hereditary breast cancer. The five-year survival statistics for metastatic disease are quite interesting, particularly when stratified by molecular subtypes. I'm considering doing my research project on tumour genetics."
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following defence mechanisms best describes this student's response?
attributes.explanation:
This case demonstrates a classic example of intellectualisation, where the medical student copes with the grief of losing her grandmother by focusing on the technical, scientific aspects of breast cancer. By converting a deeply personal and emotional loss into an academic discussion about cancer genetics, she creates emotional distance from the painful reality of her grandmother's death.

Rationalisation involves creating logical explanations for behaviours or feelings that may have other, less comfortable causes. While similar to intellectualisation, rationalisation typically involves justifying actions rather than converting them into abstract concepts.

Displacement occurs when feelings or impulses are redirected from their original target to a less threatening substitute. This would be seen if the student had directed her grief-related anger towards unrelated people or situations.

Projection involves attributing one's own unacceptable thoughts or emotions to others. This would be demonstrated if the student had accused others of being unemotional about death, rather than acknowledging her own difficulty in processing grief.

Denial involves refusing to acknowledge aspects of reality that are apparent to others. This would be seen if the student had claimed her grandmother wasn't really gone or that the death hadn't affected her at all.

Understanding defence mechanisms is crucial in clinical practice as they provide insight into how people cope with emotional distress. Intellectualisation is particularly common among healthcare professionals and can be adaptive in allowing continued function in emotionally challenging situations, but may become maladaptive if it prevents appropriate emotional processing and grief.
learningPoints: Intellectualisation is a defence mechanism where emotional or personal problems are dealt with by focusing on abstract or impersonal aspects.
Medical students and doctors commonly use intellectualisation as a defence mechanism when dealing with emotionally challenging situations.
Recognition of defence mechanisms is important for maintaining professional empathy and emotional awareness.
references: Sadock BJ, Sadock VA, Ruiz P. Kaplan and Sadock's comprehensive textbook of psychiatry. lippincott Williams & wilkins; 2017 May 11.

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
